items.rationale: Sees AMM/CFMM design as a rich, largely-open theory space and is personally producing work on it (e.g. formalizing the complexity-vs-expressivity trade-off of AMMs). Believes principled, optimal designs are within reach.
items.age_note: Sustained theme from 2021-11 through his most recent statements; weighting the 2023-03-06 work as current.

citations.quote: New work ... formalizing the intuitive trade-off between an AMM's complexity ... and its expressivity
we don't have a theory of DeFi just yet, but I'm very optimistic that we'll see a lot of interesting work over the next couple of years

items.rationale: His core research and teaching focus; treats permissionless consensus as a deep, still-developing theory and singles out DAG-based consensus as a major recent advance.

citations.quote: One of the most exciting developments in consensus protocols in 2022 has been the recent advances in DAG-based consensus.
there's a general theory of permissionless consensus ... waiting to be developed, plausibly as rich as the existing theory for the permissioned setting

items.rationale: Views SNARK proof systems as near-magical 'computational compression' and is actively excited about the race to make proving practical; central to his L2 scaling vision.

citations.quote: Excited to watch how all the SNARK-related storylines are going to play out--the suspense is killing me!
The fact that you can compress arbitrary computations in this way is shocking, IMO.

items.rationale: Frames cryptoeconomics as mechanism design with a native currency and lays out 'grand challenges' (optimal L1 incentives, macroeconomics, cross-layer incentives) he wants the field to solve.

citations.quote: Grand challenge #2: Optimal layer-1 incentives. (Holy grail: matching possibility and impossibility results ... )
This important paper shows that the breakdown of EIP-1559's incentive-compatibility ... is fundamental to the blockspace allocation problem

items.rationale: Sees blockchains as programmable virtual computers and a whole new multidisciplinary field blossoming; left academia to build a research lab around it and invests heavily in educating people about it.

citations.quote: witnessing a new multidisciplinary field (spurred by web3) blossom before our eyes. There are enormous opportunities to shape this field
a new computing paradigm—–a programmable computer that lives in the sky ... not owned by anyone and that anyone can use

items.rationale: Promotes research bridging traditional and web3 governance and favors immediately-actionable improvements to on-chain voting schemes.

citations.quote: two fascinating lectures about what web3 governance can learn from traditional governance and vice versa
immediately-actionable local improvements to existing voting schemes ... are complementary to the more ambitious non-transferable governance program

items.rationale: Repeatedly frustrated by widespread confusion about what blockchains/web3 actually are; sees the discourse as muddled and conflating unrelated questions (tech vs. behavior vs. investment).

citations.quote: conversations ... that remind me just how much confusion there currently is around web3
there's a lot of urgent work to do educating people to think clearly about web3

items.rationale: Argues the popular 'impermanent loss' measure understates the real, often-permanent losses LPs suffer to arbitrageurs; pushes LVR as the proper measure of this structural drag on liquidity provision.

citations.quote: Introducing LVR as a measure of LP loss in AMMs.
the adverse selection suffered by liquidity providers ... which typically leads to some permanent loss

items.rationale: Treats it as an open, worrying question whether MEV is fundamental to any decentralized smart-contract platform or merely an artifact of current designs—an unresolved threat to incentive cleanliness.

citations.quote: is MEV fundamental to any decentralized smart contract platform or an artifact of current designs?

items.rationale: Concerned that sloppy naming (zk-rollups without zk, impermanent loss, blockchain-as-database) corrupts understanding for future students and practitioners.

citations.quote: I have no idea why the "blockchain as DB" meme has persisted ... I hope it fades away soon.
validity rollups (often misleadingly called zk rollups, despite the lack of zero knowledge)

items.rationale: Notes a structural tension: as the block reward decays toward zero under Bitcoin's hard supply cap, undercutting/re-org attack incentives become a concern for long-run security—a design risk he flags rather than a price call.
items.age_note: Analytical/security observation, not a market or price view; he explicitly avoids investment opinions on BTC.

citations.quote: Macroeconomic policy decision (hard supply cap) => microeconomic consequences (e.g., undercutting re-org attacks as the block reward goes to 0).

items.rationale: Observes EIP-1559's fee-burning likely makes ETH deflationary long run—offered as a neutral analytical consequence of mechanism design, not as bullishness or a price view.
items.age_note: Analytical, not a price/market opinion; placed under 'concerned' only as the closest bucket for a flagged macro consequence—sentiment is genuinely neutral.

citations.quote: the EIP-1559 transaction fee mechanism ... macroeconomic consequences (ETH likely deflationary in the long run).

risk_regime.rationale: Roughgarden is a theoretical computer scientist / research lead, not a trader. He explicitly classifies which-token-to-buy questions as investment advice for a financial advisor and consistently refuses market/price opinions. His optimism is structural and technological (long-horizon excitement about the science of blockchains), not a tactical risk-on/risk-off market posture.

notes: Corpus is almost entirely academic/educational (Foundations of Blockchains lectures, AMM/CFMM and consensus theory, a16z crypto research seminars), so 'market view' here means his research-level enthusiasm and concerns, not trade positioning. Strongest and most current signals (through 2023-03) are AMM/CFMM design theory and LVR, consensus theory (incl. DAG-based consensus), SNARKs/validity rollups, and cryptoeconomics/L1 incentives. No reversals detected—views are consistent and cumulative across the 18 months; older stances (e.g. 2021 DeFi-theory optimism) persist and are reinforced by 2023 work. Token-level entries are analytical mechanism-design observations, not investment views; he deliberately abstains from price/market opinions, hence risk_regime = no_view. He explicitly distances himself from web3 'gambling' framings and emphasizes the technology is 'not about gambling' (2022-04-03).
